name: Ugayafukiaezu
description: Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto (鵜葺草葺不合尊) is a significant deity in Japanese mythology, a pivotal figure in the lineage leading to the first Emperor of Japan, Emperor Jimmu. He is considered a kami of childbirth and is often associated with the coastal regions of Japan, particularly Kyushu. His name, literally meaning "cormorant-thatch incompletely thatched deity," alludes to the circumstances of his birth. He was born in a partially built cormorant feather palace because his mother, Toyotama-hime, a dragon princess, had to return to the sea before the palace was completed. Ugayafukiaezu is the father of Emperor Jimmu's father, thus making him an important ancestor to the imperial line. He embodies the divine connection between the sea, land, and the imperial family. He is revered in various Shinto shrines, where prayers are offered for safe childbirth, prosperity, and the well-being of the nation.
subtitle: Ancestor of Emperors

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Ugayafukiaezu
keyMyths.description: Toyotama-hime, a dragon princess and wife of Hoori, retreated to a partially constructed palace made of cormorant feathers to give birth, as she reverted to her dragon form during labor. However, Hoori peeked into the birthing chamber, breaking a sacred taboo. Ashamed of being seen in her true form, Toyotama-hime abandoned her child, Ugayafukiaezu, to be raised by her younger sister, Tamayori-bime. This event marked a separation between the divine and human realms.
keyMyths.source: Kojiki, translated by Basil Hall Chamberlain

keyMyths.title: Ugayafukiaezu and Tamayori-bime
keyMyths.description: Ugayafukiaezu was raised by his aunt, Tamayori-bime. As he grew older, he eventually married her. This union produced four sons, one of whom, Hikonagisa-takeugayafukiaezu no Mikoto, became the father of Emperor Jimmu. This highlights the importance of Ugayafukiaezu in establishing the divine lineage of the imperial family and connecting them to both the sea and the dragon lineage.
keyMyths.source: Nihon Shoki, translated by W.G. Aston

keyMyths.title: The Cormorant-Thatch Palace
keyMyths.description: The incomplete cormorant-thatch palace where Ugayafukiaezu was born is symbolic of the transition between the divine and mortal realms. It represents a liminal space, a boundary between the sea and the land, reflecting the deity's role as a bridge between these two worlds. The unfinished nature of the palace also signifies the ongoing process of establishing the imperial lineage and the connection to the divine ancestors.
keyMyths.source: Aoki, Tamotsu. *An Introduction to Japanese Mythology*. Translated by Kirsten Refsing. Routledge, 2008.

sources.author: Chamberlain, Basil Hall

associations.name: Cormorant
associations.significance: Refers to the cormorant feathers used in the incomplete thatching of his birthplace.

associations.name: Sea
associations.significance: Connects him to coastal regions and his parents' divine connection to the ocean.

associations.name: Childbirth
associations.significance: He is a kami of childbirth, linking him to fertility and new beginnings.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: Indirectly linked through his parents, particularly his mother, Toyotama-hime, who is associated with lunar cycles.

associations.name: Three
associations.significance: Symbolic of the sacred number in Shinto, representing creation and harmony, reflecting his role in the imperial lineage.

associations.name: Lineage
associations.significance: Represents the divine lineage leading to the first Emperor of Japan, Emperor Jimmu.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Shares a connection to the sea and coastal regions, though Poseidon's domain is far broader and more powerful.
Like Ugayafukiaezu, Freyja has associations with fertility and lineage, although her primary domain is love and beauty.
Sobek, with his crocodilian features, embodies a connection to a specific animal and natural environment, similar to Ugayafukiaezu's link to the cormorant and the coast.

worshipPractices.name: Coastal Shrines
worshipPractices.description: Ugayafukiaezu is often enshrined in coastal shrines, particularly in Kyushu. Offerings of seafood and prayers for safe childbirth are common.

worshipPractices.name: Imperial Rituals
worshipPractices.description: As an ancestor of the imperial line, he is venerated in imperial rituals that emphasize the continuity and legitimacy of the imperial family. These rituals often involve prayers for the prosperity of the nation.

festivals.name: Autumn Harvest Festivals
festivals.description: While not exclusively dedicated to him, Ugayafukiaezu is honored during autumn harvest festivals in coastal regions, where prayers are offered for bountiful catches and the continued prosperity of fishing communities.

festivals.name: New Year Celebrations
festivals.description: Ugayafukiaezu, as an ancestor of the imperial line, is recognized during New Year celebrations, with prayers offered for the health and well-being of the imperial family and the nation.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ugayafukiaezu's legacy is primarily maintained through Shinto shrines and his role in the imperial mythology. He is a symbol of the divine ancestry of the Japanese imperial family, and his story is recounted in historical and mythological texts. His association with childbirth also makes him a figure of reverence for families.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Ugayafukiaezu's birth is steeped in mythological significance. His parents were Hoori (also known as Yamasachihiko), a descendant of the sun goddess Amaterasu, and Toyotama-hime, a dragon princess and daughter of the sea god Ryūjin. Toyotama-hime, pregnant with Ugayafukiaezu, requested to return to the land before giving birth, as she was a creature of the sea. She built a birthing hut thatched with cormorant feathers. However, she asked Hoori not to look inside during the delivery. Overcome with curiosity, Hoori peeked in and saw Toyotama-hime in her true form, a dragon. Ashamed of being seen, she returned to the sea, leaving Ugayafukiaezu behind. The name 'Ugayafukiaezu' literally translates to 'cormorant-thatch incompletely thatched deity,' a direct reference to the circumstances of his birth and the unfinished birthing hut. This unusual birth story highlights his unique position as a bridge between the divine and human realms, solidifying his importance in Japanese mythology.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Ugayafukiaezu does not feature prominently in numerous independent myths, his primary significance lies in his role within the imperial lineage. He is a crucial link in the genealogy connecting the gods to Emperor Jimmu, the legendary first emperor of Japan. His story is often recounted as part of the larger narrative of the descent of the gods and the establishment of the imperial dynasty. The circumstances of his birth, with his mother's transformation and departure, underscore the separation between the divine and human worlds, even as he embodies a connection between them. Furthermore, his lineage connects him to important symbols of Japanese mythology, such as the sea and the sun goddess, solidifying his status as a significant figure in the Shinto pantheon. His children include deities who further contribute to the establishment of the nation, ensuring his place in the foundational myths of Japan.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Ugayafukiaezu is primarily venerated within the context of Shinto shrines, particularly those located in coastal regions of Japan, especially in Kyushu. These shrines often feature rituals and ceremonies that honor his role as a kami of childbirth and as an ancestor of the imperial line. Prayers are offered for safe childbirth, healthy children, and the prosperity of the imperial family and the nation. As a deity associated with the sea, fishermen and coastal communities also pray to him for protection and bountiful catches. While there are no major festivals exclusively dedicated to Ugayafukiaezu, he is honored during broader Shinto festivals and rituals that celebrate the imperial ancestors and the blessings of nature. His veneration emphasizes the importance of lineage, fertility, and the connection between the divine and human realms in Japanese religious practices. Offerings may include seafood, rice, and sake, reflecting his association with the sea and agricultural prosperity.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Ugayafukiaezu's most enduring legacy is his role as a key figure in the lineage leading to Emperor Jimmu, the first emperor of Japan. This connection has solidified his place in Japanese mythology and cultural identity. He continues to be recognized and revered in Shinto shrines, particularly those associated with the imperial family and coastal regions. His story, while not widely known in popular culture, remains an important part of the narrative of Japan's origins and the divine ancestry of its imperial line. In modern times, Ugayafukiaezu serves as a symbol of tradition, continuity, and the enduring connection between the Japanese people and their mythological past. His association with childbirth also makes him a figure of reverence for families seeking blessings for fertility and safe delivery. He is a reminder of the complex and fascinating tapestry of Japanese mythology and its influence on the nation's cultural heritage.

shortDescription: A Japanese deity of childbirth and ancestor of Emperor Jimmu. Crucial figure in imperial mythology, linking gods and the imperial line.
